source.title: Targeted mutagenesis and high-throughput screening of diversified gene and promoter libraries for isolating gain-of-function mutations

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the bottleneck of individually testing gene and promoter variants generated by site-directed mutagenesis, which is tedious and time-intensive. The goal is to develop a method for targeted mutagenesis and high-throughput screening of diversified gene and promoter libraries to isolate gain-of-function mutations. The method combines overlap extension PCR with degenerate primers to create combinatorial libraries of 10^4-10^7 variants, which are then screened via FACS. The research process involves designing semi-rational libraries, constructing them via PCR, and screening for desired phenotypes. Key constraints include library size limitations due to transformation efficiency, primer bias, and the need for prior knowledge of target regions. The method is validated through applications in promoter optimization, biosensor construction, and enzyme engineering.

section_gists.heading: 1 Introduction
section_gists.gist: Introduces the problem: individually testing variants from site-directed mutagenesis is slow. Proposes a method using overlap extension PCR with degenerate primers to create large combinatorial libraries (10^4-10^7 variants) for high-throughput screening via FACS. Outlines the Design-Build-Test workflow.
process_signals: Motivation: eliminate bottleneck of individual variant testing
Hypothesis: combinatorial mutagenesis and high-throughput screening can rapidly isolate gain-of-function mutations
Design choice: use overlap extension PCR with degenerate primers for library generation
methods: Overlap extension PCR
Saturation/partial saturation mutagenesis with degenerate primers
FACS screening

section_gists.heading: 1.1 Promoter and RBS libraries
section_gists.gist: Discusses semi-rational design of promoter libraries, targeting specific regions like -35/-10 sites, operator regions, and RBS. Emphasizes the need for combinatorial testing and provides examples of successful applications in non-model organisms. Highlights challenges such as tuning expression for toxic gene products.
process_signals: Design principle: semi-rational targeting of known functional regions
Observation: single nucleotide mutations in -35/-10 can have dramatic effects
Constraint: need to experimentally test engineered promoters despite bioinformatic predictions
Application: tuning down constitutive promoters for stable expression of toxic genes
methods: Semi-rational mutation libraries targeting -35/-10, operator, RBS regions
failures_or_limits: Bioinformatic tools cannot fully predict promoter strength; experimental testing required

section_gists.heading: 1.2 Protein libraries
section_gists.gist: Describes rational and semi-rational approaches for protein engineering, using structural information (crystal structures, AlphaFold) and in silico docking to guide mutagenesis. Emphasizes targeting multiple positions simultaneously to explore combinatorial effects. Notes that even imperfect structures can guide effective library design.
process_signals: Design choice: use computational models to select residues for mutagenesis
Hypothesis: targeting multiple positions combinatorially accelerates gain-of-function isolation
Constraint: library size limits number of simultaneously randomized positions
Validation: successful engineering of biosensors using in silico guided libraries
methods: Rational targeted mutagenesis guided by structural data
In silico docking for residue selection
Degenerate codon primers for saturation mutagenesis
failures_or_limits: Full saturation mutagenesis of many positions impractical due to combinatorial explosion

section_gists.heading: Design
section_gists.gist: Outlines the planning and target selection process. Describes how to design degenerate primers for multiple mutation sites, fragment generation, and assembly via overlap extension PCR. Notes that the method can target several sites without significantly increasing workload, useful for biosensor engineering.
process_signals: Design strategy: separate gene into fragments with mutation sites at ends, use degenerate primers with overlaps
Hypothesis: combinatorial assembly of fragments enhances diversity
Application: successfully used to engineer a protocatechuate biosensor in P. putida
methods: Degenerate primer design with 20-24 bp overlaps
Fragment generation by PCR, assembly by overlap extension PCR
Gibson assembly for cloning

section_gists.heading: 1.3 Applications of the method
section_gists.gist: Lists successful applications: promoter optimization, ligand binding alteration, enzyme inhibition reduction, biosensor construction, enzyme efficiency increase, thermostability improvement. Demonstrates broad applicability and validation of the method.
process_signals: Validation: method applied to diverse objectives
Outcome: successful isolation of gain-of-function mutations in various contexts

section_gists.heading: 1.4 Comparisons and limitations
section_gists.gist: Section header only; no content provided.

section_gists.heading: 1.4.1 This method
section_gists.gist: Describes the core method: PCR-based site-directed mutagenesis with degenerate primers, combinatorial assembly by overlap extension PCR, and cloning by Gibson assembly. Highlights advantages: flexibility, control of mutation bias, ease of use compared to alternatives.
process_signals: Design choice: combine three established techniques for superior performance
Hypothesis: combination offers better control and flexibility than existing methods
methods: PCR with degenerate primers
Overlap extension PCR for combinatorial assembly
Gibson assembly for cloning

section_gists.heading: 1.4.2 Alternatives to rational library design
section_gists.gist: Compares to random mutagenesis (chemical, radiation, ALE). Notes that random methods require less design but produce more deleterious mutations, necessitating larger screens. Suggests that rational and random methods can complement each other.
process_signals: Trade-off: rational design reduces screening burden vs. random methods
Observation: random mutagenesis and ALE can be combined with rational libraries
methods: Random mutagenesis (chemical, radiation)
Adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE)
failures_or_limits: Random methods generate high proportion of deleterious mutations

section_gists.heading: 1.4.3 Alternative methods for targeted mutagenesis and library construction
section_gists.gist: Reviews alternative targeted mutagenesis methods (SDM, SSM, SeSaM, cepPCR, MEGAWHOP, DNA synthesis, recombineering, CRISPR-based) and their limitations: low library size, labor-intensiveness, mutation bias, high cost. Argues that the presented method offers low cost, ease of use, control of bias, and applicability in non-model organisms.
process_signals: Constraint: existing methods have limitations in library size, cost, or bias
Design rationale: choose PCR-based method for low cost and simplicity
Comparison: method allows combinatorial and targeted mutagenesis without expensive synthesis
methods: SDM, SSM, SeSaM, cepPCR, MEGAWHOP, DNA synthesis, recombineering, CREATE, ICM
failures_or_limits: SDM/SSM: low library size, not combinatorial
cepPCR: mutation bias, limited to shorter sequences
CRISPR methods: high cost for oligo synthesis

section_gists.heading: 1.4.4 Limitations of this method
section_gists.gist: Explicitly states limitations: degenerate primers may introduce bias due to differential annealing; verification by sequencing is required. Library size limited by transformation efficiency (10^5-10^6). Requires prior knowledge of target regions. Screening throughput limited by FACS capacity.
process_signals: Failure mode: primer bias can skew library representation
Mitigation: include verification step (sequencing random clones or whole library)
Constraint: transformation efficiency caps library size
Constraint: need for prior knowledge of functional regions
methods: Verification by sequencing random clones or whole library
failures_or_limits: Primer bias due to differential annealing
Library size limited by transformation efficiency

section_gists.heading: 2 Materials
section_gists.gist: Section header only; no content provided.

section_gists.heading: 3.1 Overview
section_gists.gist: Provides a timeline for the protocol: ~3 weeks from design to clone isolation. Details daily steps: PCR, gel extraction, transformation, colony collection, FACS sorting (multiple rounds with increasing stringency), and individual clone characterization. Notes strategies like collecting top 5% in first sort, negative sorting to eliminate constitutive performers.
process_signals: Process: iterative sorting with increasing stringency
Observation: need for negative sorting to remove constitutive performers
Design choice: collect top 5% initially to capture rare clones
methods: Multi-round FACS sorting
Negative sorting to eliminate constitutive variants

section_gists.heading: 3.2 Primer design and construction
section_gists.gist: Briefly describes primer design for a hypothetical library with two mutation sites. Notes that mutation sites can range from single base to a stretch covered by a primer, considering 3' and 5' overlaps.
process_signals: Design constraint: mutation site size limited by primer length and overlap requirements
methods: Primer design with 3' overlap for initial PCR and 5' overlap for overlap extension PCR

section_gists.heading: 3.2.1 PCR based mutagenesis: (1 day)
section_gists.gist: Details PCR setup: for two mutation sites, six primers needed (degenerate and non-variable). Notes that sometimes multiple degenerate primers are needed to avoid unwanted codon combinations. PCR conditions: lower annealing temperatures for degenerate primers. Gel extraction of correct bands.
process_signals: Technical challenge: degenerate primers may require multiple primers to avoid unwanted codons
Optimization: lower annealing temperature for degenerate primers
Quality control: gel extraction to isolate correct fragments
methods: PCR with degenerate primers
Agarose gel electrophoresis and extraction
failures_or_limits: Potential for unwanted codon combinations if single degenerate primer used

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from the need to accelerate gain-of-function mutation isolation by combining combinatorial mutagenesis with high-throughput screening.
The method was developed by integrating established techniques (degenerate primers, overlap PCR, Gibson assembly) to overcome limitations of existing methods (low throughput, high cost, bias).
Initial trials likely revealed primer bias and transformation efficiency limits, leading to inclusion of verification steps and library size recommendations.
The protocol was refined through iterative FACS sorting with increasing stringency and negative selection to eliminate false positives.
Validation was achieved through diverse applications, demonstrating broad utility despite acknowledged limitations.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Exact nature of primer bias and its impact on library representation
Specific transformation efficiency limits for different hosts
Details of optimization for PCR conditions (e.g., annealing temperature gradients)
Frequency of unwanted mutations or assembly errors
Quantitative comparison of screening efficiency vs. alternative methods

events.summary: Individually testing single mutations is tedious and time-intensive, creating a bottleneck.
Combinatorial testing of multiple mutations in a large library can eliminate the bottleneck.
Method combines overlap extension PCR with degenerate primers to create combinatorial libraries of 10^4-10^7 variants.
Libraries are screened via FACS with a fluorescent reporter for high-throughput isolation of variants.
Semi-rational design targets specific promoter regions (-35/-10, operator, RBS) for mutagenesis.
Bioinformatic tools cannot fully predict promoter strength; experimental testing is still necessary.
Protein engineering uses structural data (crystal structures, AlphaFold) and in silico docking to guide targeted mutagenesis.
Full saturation mutagenesis of many positions is impractical due to combinatorial explosion.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk introduces the core problem, proposed solution, and design principles for promoter and protein library construction.

events.summary: Design of protein mutation library involves targeting residues for mutagenesis around the area of interest, separating the protein sequence into fragments with mutation sites at ends, and using degenerate primers for PCR amplification.
Use of BLOSUM62 substitution matrix to guide conservative mutations for generating variants that are dissimilar but not entirely divergent.
Computational models like Rosetta or AlphaFold predict tertiary structures, and in silico docking guides selection of amino acids for mutagenesis, reducing library size and workload.
In silico ligand docking in a comparative model successfully guided design of focused libraries for engineering biosensors for small molecules.
The method combines PCR-based site-directed mutagenesis with degenerate primers, overlap extension PCR, and Gibson assembly for superior flexibility and control of mutation bias.
Alternative random mutagenesis methods produce a larger percentage of deleterious mutations, requiring screening from a larger population.
Existing targeted mutagenesis methods like SDM and SSM have low library sizes, restricting their application in directed evolution.
DNA synthesis for library generation is still expensive compared to other techniques, limiting its application.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers design principles, computational guidance, method combination, and limitations of alternatives.

events.summary: Degenerate primers may introduce bias due to differential annealing during PCR.
Protocol includes a verification step by sequencing random clones or whole library to ensure desired mutations and sufficient diversity.
Method requires prior knowledge of the targeted protein or promoter.
Primer design for two mutation sites requires six primers: degenerate primers with 20 bp overlaps, non-variable reverse primers, and flanking primers.
Protocol timeline: ~3 weeks from design to clone isolation for a library of 10^5-10^6 variants.
First round of FACS sorting collects top 5% to capture rare clones; negative sorting recommended to eliminate constitutive performers.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers limitations, primer design details, and protocol overview with specific steps and recommendations.

events.summary: PCR amplification of fragments with lowered annealing temperatures for degenerate primers.
Gel extraction of correct-sized PCR fragments.
Overlap extension PCR to assemble fragments into full-length mutated constructs.
Cloning of assembled library into vector via Gibson assembly or restriction/ligation.
Verification of library diversity by sequencing individual clones or mixed population.
Expected results show broad phenotypic distribution with rare gain-of-function variants in the high fluorescence tail.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed protocol steps (design, PCR, assembly, transformation, verification) and expected results analysis, directly supporting the paper's method development and validation.

events.summary: The method's efficacy is validated by several original research papers that successfully employed this approach.
The method eliminates the bottleneck of testing individual variants by assaying combinatorial effects of several mutations at once.
The combination of overlap extension PCR, degenerate primers, and FACS is unique to this methodology.
High-throughput screening allows millions of variants to be screened in a single sample tube, with FACS isolating good performers.
The method was developed and tested for protein engineering, biosensor development, and metabolic engineering in multiple microbial strains.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains the conclusion and author contributions, providing validation and summary of the method's advantages and applications.
This chunk contains only a reference list with no research process events.

evidence_bank.quote: introducing single mutations one at a time and individually testing for changes in function is a tedious and time-intensive process

The protocol described herein eliminates the bottleneck of individually testing variants of genes and promoters (generated by site-directed mutagenesis) by assaying the combinatorial effect of several mutations at once, as part of a large multi-variant library.

The method described here uses a combination of overlap extension Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) (Ho et al., 1989; Horton et al., 1989) and saturation or partial saturation mutagenesis with degenerate primers (Kretz et al., 2004) to produce a library of gene and promoter variants

The resulting libraries lend themselves to high-throughput screening via fluorescence activated cell sorting (FACS), when coupled to a relevant fluorescent reporter, allowing for the rapid identification and isolation of variants of interest

semi-rational refers to the approach of targeting specific regions of the promoter, known to be involved with various mechanisms of transcription or translation, rather than just randomly mutating the entire promoter region.
it is still necessary to test engineered promoters experimentally

When targeting which sequences to randomize, one may take either a random, semi-rational or fully rational approach. This is frequently determined by the amount of information available for the protein of interest

saturation mutagenesis is only used to fully randomize a few positions in the protein sequence simultaneously, otherwise, the number of possible combinations of variants quickly becomes impractical to build individually in the laboratory or test in a given timeline
A number of residues are targeted for mutagenesis around the area of interest
The protein sequence is then separated into several fragments with the mutation sites located at one end of each fragment.

Each fragment will be generated by PCR reactions consisting of the template to be mutated and a pair of primers. One of those primers (the mutation primer) includes degenerate bases in the middle to effect mutation.
When designing the primers, it is helpful to refer to an amino acid substitution matrix, such as the Block Substitution Matrix 62 (BLOSUM62) table

In the case of the BLOSUM62 matrix, the scores shown were determined based on sequences with an identity of 62% or less – making it useful for generating variants that are dissimilar to the starting sequence, but not entirely divergent
Tertiary structures can also be predicted for proteins with a known sequence using computational approaches such as Rosetta (Rohl et al., 2004) or AlphaFold (Jumper et al., 2021).
In silico docking consisting of protein-protein or protein-ligand interactions (Lyskov and Gray, 2008; Lyskov et al., 2013) permits guided selection of amino acids involved in binding.

Targeting only very relevant residues with computationally informed mutations, or conservative mutations such as neutral drift mutations (Lynch and Hill, 1986), can simplify library generation, reduce library size, and decrease noise and workload.

In silico ligand docking in a comparative model to guide the design of a focused library was successfully used to engineer several biosensors for small molecules (Jha et al., 2015; Jha et al., 2016; Shin et al., 2022).

The method described here introduces a variety of mutations at each mutation site by performing PCR-based, site directed mutagenesis, with primers containing degenerate bases at the desired mutation site.
Diversity is then further enhanced by the combinatorial assembly of those fragments by overlap extension PCR
The resulting gene or promoter library is then cloned into a vector by Gibson assembly
their combination is rare within the mutation library field, despite offering superior flexibility, control of mutation bias, and ease of use

Without designing a bias towards potentially useful mutations, it is expected that a much larger percentage of the mutations will be deleterious, which requires screening or selecting from a larger sampling of the population.

Targeted mutagenesis approaches, such as site directed mutagenesis (SDM) and site-saturation mutagenesis (SSM) have proven to be extremely useful, however, the relatively low library size that can be achieved through these methods has restricted their application in directed evolution approaches
Still, the high price, compared to other techniques, makes this technique appropriate for only some specific applications.
the different sequences may show differences in annealing during PCR resulting in a degree of bias

this protocol includes a verification step of either sequencing a random selection of clones isolated from the library (see Figure 6A), or sequencing DNA extracted from the whole library (see Figure 6B), for quality assurance
This method is additionally limited by availability of knowledge about the targeted protein or promoter

For our example of two mutation sites, a total of six primers are needed; one degenerate primer at each mutation site with 20 base pair overlaps that extend into adjacent fragments, one non-variable primer for each mutation site to serve as the reverse primer for that fragment and finally, two primers to flank the entire region.
In total, this method will take approximately 3 weeks from initial library design to isolating and characterizing individual clones, for a reasonable library size of $10^{5}-10^{6}$
in the first round of sorting it is recommended to collect the top 5% of the library
A negative sorting is recommended to eliminate constitutive performers, especially observed when engineering regulatory proteins and promoters
For primers with degenerate bases, annealing temperatures should be lowered to accommodate the mutation variant with the least stable hybridization to the template.
The entire PCR product is then run on an agarose gel and the correct sized bands are excised.
Extract DNA from the gel excisions using commercially available gel extraction kits (e.g., QiaQuick gel extraction kit)

PCR fragments are then combined via overlap extension PCR. In the first stage of overlap PCR, the fragments (at equimolar ratio) and PCR reagents/enzymes are allowed to react for 8 cycles with an extension time sufficient to copy the largest fragment. Then, primers flanking the entire promoter region are introduced and 25 more cycles are performed with an extension time sufficient for the entire region.
The mutated variant library is then assembled into a linearized vector using Gibson assembly (Gibson et al., 2009; Thomas et al., 2015) or restriction digestion/ligation.

In order to verify the integrity and diversity of the library, plasmid DNA is isolated from individual clones (e.g., picked from the quantification plate in step 7 and grown up overnight) or from a small volume of the collected library, using any desired plasmid DNA extraction method (i.e., QiaGen Miniprep Kit). The isolated DNA, along with an appropriate primer, is sent for sequencing by one's preferred provider (in-house, Twist, Eurofins, etc.).

In the example histogram provided (Figure 6C), the majority of mutation combinations for a promoter library are deleterious, causing the average fluorescence to go down. The strength of the library lies in the rare mutations with gain of function or improved performance, evident in the high fluorescence tail of the library population which extends slightly beyond the high fluorescence region of the wild type population.

The efficacy of the method described here is illustrated by several of our original research papers (Jha et al., 2014; Jha et al., 2016; Jha et al., 2018; Shin et al., 2022), which have successfully employed this approach.

The methodology described here eliminates the bottleneck of testing individual variants of genes and promoters by presenting a protocol for assaying the combinatorial effect of several mutations at once, drastically reducing the time and money required to obtain genetic variants with desirable qualities.

While the individual components of this workflow (overlap extension PCR, degenerate primers, FACS, etc.) may seem commonplace, we have not encountered any other methodology that combines them in the fashion outlined in this paper.

millions of variants can conveniently be screened for desirable phenotypes in a single sample tube. Variants that perform well are isolated by FACS and screened further in subsequent rounds, while poor performers are easily discarded.

All authors developed and tested the method for various protein engineering, biosensor development and metabolic engineering efforts in several microbial strains, including E. coli, P. putida and A. baylyi.
